name: Mimisbrunnr
description: Mimisbrunnr, meaning "Mimir's Well" or "Mimir's Spring," is a sacred well in Norse mythology associated with wisdom and knowledge. It is located beneath one of the roots of the world tree Yggdrasil, specifically the root that extends into Jotunheim, the land of the giants. The well is guarded by Mimir, a wise being often depicted as a giant or possibly a god, who possesses profound knowledge and understanding of the universe. The water of Mimisbrunnr is believed to be the source of all wisdom, and those who drink from it gain immense insight. Odin, the Allfather, famously sacrificed one of his eyes to Mimir in exchange for a single drink from the well, demonstrating the extraordinary value placed on its wisdom. This act signifies Odin's relentless pursuit of knowledge and his willingness to make significant sacrifices to attain it. The well's connection to Yggdrasil further reinforces its importance, as the world tree links all nine realms of Norse cosmology. Mimisbrunnr is not merely a source of information but a mystical place where profound truths are revealed, accessible only through sacrifice and dedication. The well's existence highlights the Norse emphasis on wisdom as a valuable asset, often sought at great personal cost. It is a place of immense power and profound significance in the Norse worldview, representing the ultimate source of knowledge and understanding.
subtitle: Well of Wisdom beneath Yggdrasil

keyMyths.title: Odin's Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: Odin sacrifices one of his eyes to Mimir in exchange for a drink from Mimisbrunnr, gaining immense wisdom. This shows his dedication to knowledge.

keyMyths.title: Mimir's Head
keyMyths.description: After the Aesir-Vanir war, Mimir's head is sent to Odin. Odin preserves it with herbs and magic, allowing Mimir to continue advising him from the well.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A key source for Norse mythology, including details about Mimisbrunnr and Odin's sacrifice.
